data.title: Is BP (LSE:BP.) Pricing Reflecting Its Strong Recent Share Performance?
data.description:

  • Recent attention on BP has been shaped by broader energy sector news and ongoing investor interest in large integrated oil companies. This often shifts how the market prices long term prospects and risks. These headlines provide useful context for thinking about whether BP's recent price performance aligns with its underlying worth.
  • BP currently scores 5 out of 6 on our valuation checks for being potentially undervalued. You can see the full breakdown via this valuation score of 5. Approach 1: BP Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those amounts back to today using a required rate of return.

For BP, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about US$10.7b. Analysts provide explicit free cash flow estimates out to 2030, including a projection of US$17.4b in that year, with further years extrapolated by Simply Wall St to extend the cash flow path.

When those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$19.29 per share. Compared with the current share price of £5.29, the DCF output implies a 72.6% discount, which points to the shares trading well below this cash flow based estimate of value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

For companies that are generating meaningful revenue, the P/S ratio is a useful way to see how much investors are currently paying for each unit of sales. It is less affected by one off items in earnings, so it can be a cleaner cross check alongside cash flow based estimates.

In general, higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk tend to support a higher P/S multiple, while lower growth and higher risk usually justify a lower multiple. That context helps when you compare BP’s figures with other Oil and Gas companies.

BP currently trades on a P/S of 0.58x. This sits well below both the Oil and Gas industry average of 2.30x and the peer group average of 1.98x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 2.02x for BP, which is the P/S multiple suggested by factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, size, industry and company specific risks.

data.title: Eli Lilly’s China Bet On Orforglipron Reshapes Obesity Drug Narrative
data.description:
• Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) announced a US$3b, decade-long plan to localize production and expand supply chain capacity for its oral obesity drug candidate, orforglipron, in China.
  • The company is partnering with Pharmaron for manufacturing and has filed orforglipron with Chinese regulators as part of an integrated, China-focused value chain.

Eli Lilly, known for treatments in diabetes, obesity care and other chronic conditions, is pushing deeper into obesity medicine with oral GLP-1 candidates such as orforglipron. The new China-focused investment comes amid strong global interest in weight management drugs and broader efforts by pharmaceutical companies to secure reliable production for high-demand therapies.

For you as an investor, this move highlights how Eli Lilly is tying manufacturing, partnerships and regulatory filings together in a single market entry plan. The scale and length of the commitment in China may influence how you think about the company’s international footprint, exposure to Asian healthcare markets and potential execution risks around a large, multi-year build out.

📰 Beyond the headline: 2 risks and 4 things going right for Eli Lilly that every investor should see.

This US$3b, decade-long commitment in China signals that Eli Lilly is treating orforglipron as a core global product rather than a side bet. By localizing oral solid-dose manufacturing and locking in capacity through Pharmaron, Lilly is trying to reduce supply risk and shorten distribution routes for China and potentially other Asian markets. For you, the key point is that this is not just about one drug; it is a build-out of a China-focused value chain that could later support other small-molecule medicines. At the same time, it adds execution questions, including regulatory timelines, local partner performance, and policy shifts that could affect foreign pharma in China. The move sits alongside Lilly’s U.S. access work for GLP-1 drugs, such as Employer Connect, so you are seeing both demand-side and supply-side investments around obesity care. How well Lilly balances capital going into China, Korea Gateway Labs and Japanese capacity with legal and pricing pressures at home will influence how durable this obesity-focused business model looks over time.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Chewy is 26.9x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what P/E the company might trade on given its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and key risks. It aims to be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry because it directly incorporates those company specific factors.

Compared with this Fair Ratio of 26.9x, the current P/E of 49.5x suggests the shares screen as overvalued on this metric.
